scenarios.task: We're switching to a new supplier for a key component because their quoted unit price is 8% lower than our current supplier. That's a clear, straightforward cost win for the business.
criteria: Flags evaluating the switch on unit price alone as the optimizing-for-unit-price-alone failure mode
Recommends checking total cost of ownership — quality/defect rate, delivery reliability, switching costs — before concluding this is a net win
States that a cheaper unit price from a less reliable or lower-quality supplier can cost more overall once these factors are included
Doesn't reject the switch outright — the issue is deciding based on price alone without the total-cost comparison

scenarios.task: In our annual contract renewal negotiation with a long-term strategic supplier we depend on heavily, I pushed hard for the maximum possible price concession and got it, using our leverage as their largest customer. I consider this a clear win for our department this quarter.
criteria: Questions whether maximally extracting concessions from a long-term, depended-upon supplier is actually the right move, naming the purely-adversarial-negotiation-on-an-ongoing-relationship failure mode
States that damaging the relationship's durability and goodwill in exchange for a one-time price gain can cost more than it saves over the life of an ongoing relationship
Recommends understanding the supplier's actual constraints and seeking terms that work for both parties where the relationship has real ongoing value
Distinguishes a legitimate hard negotiation from one that damages a relationship the organization depends on long-term

scenarios.task: We did a thorough financial health and risk assessment on a key supplier three years ago when we onboarded them, and everything checked out fine. We haven't re-evaluated their risk profile since then since the onboarding process was rigorous.
criteria: Flags the lack of any reassessment since onboarding as the one-time-supplier-risk-assessment failure mode
States that a supplier's financial health, capacity, and geographic/geopolitical exposure can change materially after initial vetting
Recommends periodic ongoing reassessment of supplier risk, not treating the onboarding evaluation as sufficient indefinitely
Notes that three years is enough time for a meaningful change in supplier risk profile to have occurred unnoticed

scenarios.task: In this contract negotiation, I focused almost all our effort on getting the best possible unit price, which we achieved. The delivery penalty clause and quality guarantee terms are fairly weak and generic — we didn't push hard on those since the price was the main thing that mattered to us.
criteria: Flags spending negotiation effort primarily on price while accepting weak failure-scenario terms as the failure mode
States that delivery penalty and quality guarantee terms determine the real protection available when something goes wrong, and deserve comparable negotiation rigor to price
Recommends renegotiating or strengthening the failure-scenario terms, not treating the price win as sufficient
Frames this as misallocating negotiation effort relative to where real risk and value actually live
